coredata.dc:title: Simple chemometrics estimation of absorptivity and concentration of silver nanoparticles
coredata.dc:creator: Shariati-Rad, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: A new method was introduced for determination of absorptivity and subsequent determination of concentration and size of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Without using any complicated instrument, based on a kinetic reaction of AgNPs, a ratio curve is extracted and chemometrically fitted to obtain the absorptivity of the AgNPs. Afterwards, it can be possible to estimate size of the AgNPs based on the reported relations. The method can be simply used for any NPs provided that it can absorb in UV–Vis region of the electromagnetic spectrum and after employing in a kinetic reaction. The agreement between the calculated diameter of the AgNPs based on the absorptivity derived from the method and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging were excellent. It can be claimed that this is the most direct method to determine NPs absorptivity and concentration.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver nanoparticles
Absorptivity
Concentration
Chemometrics
Kinetic

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Theory and principle of the method
Hard-modelling for calculation of absorptivity and concentration
Estimation of the full absorptivity of the AgNPs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectral changes after addition of (a) cefixime (1.10 ​× ​10−5 ​M) to the solution of AgNP in the 0–40 ​min in 1 ​min intervals, (b) thiosulfate (2.87 ​× ​10−5 ​M) to the solution of AgNP in the 0–30 ​min in 1 ​min intervals, (c) sulfide (1.85 ​× ​10−5 ​M) to the solution of AgNP in the 0–20 ​min in 1 ​min intervals, (d) dithionite (2.87 ​× ​10−5 ​M) to the solution of AgNP in the 0–20 ​min in 1 ​min intervals and (e) dopamine (1.00 ​× ​10−5 ​M) to the solution of AgNP in the 0–10 ​min in 1 ​min intervals. The arrows show the direction of absorbance variations with time.
Variation of r with time for the reaction of synthesized AgNP with dithionite.
Estimated spectral profiles for the kinetic reaction of AgNPs with (a) cefixime, (b) thiosulfate, (c) sulfide, (d) dithionite and (e) dopamine.
TEM image of (a) AgNP-1, (b) AgNP-2 and (c) AgNP-3.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: d
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Diameter of AgNPs calculated by absorptivity data and relations proposed by Navarro and Werts [34].

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: e
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Diameter of AgNPs estimated by TEM imaging.

head.ce:title: Simple chemometrics estimation of absorptivity and concentration of silver nanoparticles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Group of Design and Fabrication of Kit, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Group of Design and Fabrication of Kit

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Group of Design and Fabrication of Kit, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Department of Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, Razi University, Kermanshah, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new method was introduced for determination of absorptivity and subsequent determination of concentration and size of silver nanoparticles (AgNPs). Without using any complicated instrument, based on a kinetic reaction of AgNPs, a ratio curve is extracted and chemometrically fitted to obtain the absorptivity of the AgNPs. Afterwards, it can be possible to estimate size of the AgNPs based on the reported relations. The method can be simply used for any NPs provided that it can absorb in UV–Vis region of the electromagnetic spectrum and after employing in a kinetic reaction. The agreement between the calculated diameter of the AgNPs based on the absorptivity derived from the method and the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) imaging were excellent. It can be claimed that this is the most direct method to determine NPs absorptivity and concentration.

ce:section.ce:para: Silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have been widely used in detection and determination systems [1–16]. In most cases, the analysis is based on the surface Plasmon resonance (SPR) band of these nanoparticles in visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum.

AgNPs in these analytical applications acts as a reagent. In analytical chemistry, addition of a specified and known amount of the reagent in a determination system is important. In most cases, the amount of the reagent is expressed as molar concentration. Therefore, knowledge about the concentration of NPs like AgNPs is very important.

Different methods and procedures have been introduced for determination of concentration of AgNPs. However, most of these techniques require many assumptions and information and even require special instrumentation. For example, Mie calculations are the basis of the many of the reports in this area [17–19]. Mie calculations are performed based on a theory which naturally may deviate from the real data.

Gravimetric methods comprise a second group of the methods [20]. In these methods, it is needed to know the number of NPs in the examined colloidal solution (N), which itself is calculated using number of atoms in a unit NPs (Nparticle). In order to calculate Nparticle, diameter of the NPs is required [21]. This means that for precise determinations, transmission electron micrograph (TEM) of the NPs must be taken.

Paramelle et al. [22] proposed a rapid method based on UV–Vis spectrum for determination of size of AgNPs. However, the requirement of their method is the knowledge about the size and the crystalline structure of the NPs. One of the methods for size determination of NPs is TEM. Determination of size of NPs by TEM is accurate and NPs can be seen directly. However, the involved instrumentation cannot be found anywhere. Moreover, the estimated diameter is based on a fraction of the NPs population [23].

Also, quartz crystal microgravimetry was employed by Reipa et al. [24] to determine NPs concentration. In this method, the shift in the frequency of the crystal resonance is recorded after adding the desired solution and solvent evaporation. The requirement of the method is a calibration graph with standards. Moreover, the instrumentation is not available elsewhere and cannot be used routinely.

Gadogbe et al. [25] proposed a method based on ligand adsorption for estimation of diameter and subsequent determination of concentration of AuNPs. The method needs packing density on AuNPs with the specific ligand. Moreover, density of the AuNPs which requires the knowledge about the crystalline structure of AuNPs is needed. As stated by Gadogbe et al. [25], there is not an agreement for a particular AuNP standard to determine ligand packing density, precisely.

Notwithstanding the numerous methods for estimation of absorptivity, concentration and size of NPs, there is still a need for more accurate and simpler methods. Accurate and robust methods which have experimental support are needed. The method introduced here is based on the real data and a fitting method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theory and principle of the method
ce:section.ce:para: Consider the kinetic reaction between components A and B with the product P by rate constant of k. If the reaction is an elementary reaction, the order of the reaction can be second-order or pseudo-first order with regard to the concentration ratio of the components.

During the kinetic reaction, it can be possible to record e.g. UV–Vis spectra with time which can form a matrix like X with n(times) ​× ​m(wavelengths) dimensions. Because of the much higher concentration of B relative to A (AgNP), and the known concentration of B, hard-modelling [26–30] can be used to find the second-order rate constant of the reaction between A and B. In these conditions, rate constant can be calculated correctly but the concentration and spectral profile of A (AgNP) are calculated ambiguously. In the case where component B absorbs in the examined wavelength range, its contribution to the X matrix can be eliminated by knowing the rate constant and its spectrum through:
(1)
X r
= X −
c B
s B

Concentration profile of B (
c B
) can be calculated by
(2)
c B
=
C
0 B
e
− k t
where
C
0 B
is the initial concentration of B. The residual data matrix (Xr) now depicts a first-order reaction (A→ B). By dividing the first spectra in Xr (which is now the spectrum of A (AgNP)), to the other spectra (in different times) ratio spectra can be obtained. Based on the Beer law, ratio spectra in a single wavelength (
r
( t )
) can be written as:
(3)
r
( t )
=
A A
A A
+
A P
=
ε A
C
A t 0
ε A
C
A t
+
ε P
C
P t
=
ε A
C 0
e
− k
t 0
ε A
C 0
e
− k t
+
ε P
C
P t
=
ε A
C 0
e
− k
t 0
ε A
C 0
e
− k t
+
ε P
C 0
(
1 −
e
− k t
)
where
C 0
is the initial concentration of A (AgNP) and C is the concentration of the components indicated with the indices at time t. After cancelation and rearrangement, we have
(4)
r
( t )
=
ε A
(
ε A
−
ε P
)
e
− k t
+
ε P
ε A
and
ε P
are the molar absorptivity of A (AgNP) and the final product, respectively. The above function can be fitted for two parameters
ε A
and
ε P
by hard-modelling. After that, using Beer law and the absorbance of A at the desired wavelength, concentration of A (AgNP) can be obtained. Here, hard-modelling based on the Newton-Gauss algorithm modified by Levenberg and Marquardt (NGL/M) was employed [26]. In order to obtain reliable results, initial guess of the asked parameters should not be far from the true parameters.

As another approach, using Eq. (3), for estimating
ε A
and
ε P
, these parameters can be swept in the suitable suspected region. Then for each value of
ε A
and
ε P
inserted in Eq. (3) a r(t) vector is calculated. Difference of this vector and experimental r(t) will resulted in a residual vector e(t) which sum over all of the its squared elements gives ssq:
(5)
S S Q = r
( t )
e x p
− r
( t )
c a l c

This results in a matrix for SSQ. The true examined
ε A
and
ε P
values during sweeping gave minimum ssq in matrix SSQ.

All of the chemicals were of analytical reagent grade. Chemicals including silver nitrate, ethanol, ammonia, sodium thiosulfate and sodium dithionite were purchased from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Dopamine and cefixime were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. Sodium sulfide was prepared from BDH chemicals Ltd Poole England. UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded by an Agilent 4553 spectrophotometer in a 1 ​cm path-length quartz cell. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were taken by transmission electron microscope of Zeiss Ultra (Oberkochen, Germany).

Single-step hydrothermal method was employed to prepare CDs using apple, walnut bark and apricot [31,32]. Separately, 15.0 ​g of apple, walnut bark, apricot were weighed and boiled in 100 ​mL distilled water for 10 ​min. The resultant mixture was crushed. After filtering, the filtrate was mixed with 20 ​mL of ethanol and heated for at 150 ​°C. After 3 ​h heating, a dark brown solution was obtained. After filtration of the solution through a Whatman filter paper, a homogeneous solution containing CDs was obtained that was used in the assay. For obtaining solid state of CD, the solvent was evaporated.

For synthesis of AgNPs, synthesized CDs were used as reducing and stabilizing agent. For this purpose, in each case, to a 100 ​mL boiling distilled water, 125 ​μL of the synthesized CDs with concentration of 50 ​mg/mL was added and boiled for 15 ​min while stirring. In the next step, after injection of 1 ​mL NH3 (10%), 5 ​mL of AgNO3 (3.42 ​mg/mL) was added. After proceeding the reaction for 45 ​min, the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature. For removing the unreacted silver nitrate, the final mixture was ultra-filtrated with a 10-kDa membrane for obtaining AgNPs.

Kinetic data during the reaction between synthesized AgNPs and different species can be observed in Fig. 1 . Synthesized AgNPs have maximum absorbance at about 410–430 ​nm. After addition of reactant species, absorbances of the AgNPs in the overall region of recording (330–550 ​nm) diminish with time. In the reaction with cefixime, thiosulfate and sulfide (Fig. 1a, b and 1c), the same AgNP was used except that for reaction with sulfide instead of 1.0 ​mL of AgNP, 0.5 ​mL of the synthesized AgNP was used in the reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hard-modelling for calculation of absorptivity and concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to calculate the absorptivity of the synthesized AgNPs, the method proposed in this work was applied. Firstly, based on a first-order kinetic model, the first-order rate constants were obtained. Secondly, ratio spectra can be obtained and after inserting the calculated first-order rate constants (k) in Eq. (4), hard-modelling using NGL/M algorithm was employed. Calculated r(t) contains two parameters (
ε A
and
ε P
). Hard-modelling resulted in the absorptivities reported in Table 1 . By employing Beer law at the maxima of the absorption spectra of the synthesized AgNPs, concentration of AgNP were also calculated and reported in Table 1. Typical fitted and experimental r(t) plot has been shown in Fig. 2 .

It is clear from Table 1 that the calculated
ε A
values for the reaction with cefixime, thiosulfate and sulfide are very close to one another. It must be mentioned that the products are agglomerated shape of the AgNP with the bridges constructed by the reacting species (cefixime, thiosulfate or sulfide). By inserting
ε A
and absorbance of the AgNPs at corresponding maxima in Beer law, concentration of the AgNPs have been calculated and reported in Table 1. In the analyzed reaction with sulfide, concentration of AgNP was calculated as 5.58 ​× ​10−10 ​M. In this reaction, 0.5 ​mL of the synthesized AgNP was used instead of 1.0 ​mL which was used in the reaction with cefixime and thiosulfate. Therefore, the obtained concentration for AgNP in the case of sulfide is closely equal to the half (0.46) of the concentration for AgNP in the reaction with cefixime and thiosulfate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Estimation of the full absorptivity of the AgNPs
ce:section.ce:para:
Now we have second-order rate constant for the reaction between AgNPs and the examined species. Moreover, concentrations of AgNPs were estimated. Therefore, it is possible to calculate the concentration profiles (matrix C) of the all species in the reaction: AgNP ​+ ​B → P, where B and P resemble the reactant species (cefixime, sulfide, thiosulfate, dithionite and dopamine) and the product, respectively. Now, by knowing the kinetic spectral data (X) and C, the true spectra of the contributing species to the above reaction can be calculated by simple least squares:
(6)
S =
(
C T
C
)
− 1
C T
X

Spectral profiles contained in S are comprised of absorptivity of the species including AgNPs. This means that the absorptivity of AgNPs in all of the examined wavelengths has been obtained. For the reactions explored here, the spectral profiles were estimated and were shown in Fig. 3 .

For estimation of the size of the synthesized AgNPs, the relations between absorptivity and size (diameter) of the AgNPs reported by Navarro and Werts [34] was used:
(7)
ε = 2.3 ×
10 5
×
d 3.48
(8)
ε = 4.2 ×
10 8
×
d 0.77

These relations were originally derived for estimation of absorptivity of AgNPs based on their diameter (
d ≤ 38
n m
and
d > 38
n m
, respectively). Based on these relations, diameters of the synthesized AgNPs were calculated (Table 1).

In order to confirm the results of the proposed method, TEM image of the synthesized AgNPs were prepared (Fig. 4 ). The analysis of the images showed that the mean diameters of the particles are 9.2, 43.2 and 44.2 ​nm for AgNP-1, AgNP-2 and AgNP-3, respectively. Comparison of these sizes with those estimated by Eqs. (7) and (8) based on the calculated absorptivities confirms reliability of the proposed method for calculation of absorptivity of AgNPs.

A chemometrics approach based on a kinetic reaction was introduced for calculation of the absorptivity of AgNPs and subsequent determination of corresponding concentration. The method is also recommended for determination of absorptivity and concentration of nanoparticles like AuNPs. The results showed that the method proposed for determination of absorptivity and subsequent calculation of concentration and size of the AgNPs is very efficient.
Masoud Shariati-Rad: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The author do not have any conflict of interest.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.chemolab.2020.103983.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Selective chromogenic detection of thiol-containing biomolecules using carbonaceous nanospheres loaded with silver nanoparticles as carrier

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.-C. Jin, Z.-Q. Xu, P. Dong, L. Lai, J.-Y. Lan, F.-L. Jiang, Y. Liu, One-step synthesis of silver nanoparticles using carbon dots as reducing and stabilizing agentsand their antibacterial mechanisms , Carbon 94 (2015) 129-141.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Resonant light scattering spectroscopy of gold, silver and gold–silver alloy nanoparticles and optical detection in microfluidic channels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.R.G. Navarro, M.H.V. Werts, Resonant light scattering spectroscopy of gold, silver and gold-silver alloy nanoparticles and optical detection in microfluidic channels, Analyst 138 (2013) 583-592.
